speaker: interviewer
text: I'm conducting an interview with Rabbi Leslie Hardman. We're in London, England, and this is tape number two.

speaker: interviewee
text: We crossed the Rhine, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and as we were moving, moving quite speedily into the interior of Germany, I found myself doing nothing else but moving with the trucks. We were at rear headquarters, we were not in the fighting unit. I was doing nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is 1944. Because I arrived-- yes, I arrived in-- in Holland before then, September, just after the Jewish holidays. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Yes, well, do you want me to talk about my work in-- in Holland? Or you want me--

speaker: interviewer
text: Just a little bit about it, so you can describe how you actually got to Belsen eventually.

speaker: interviewee
text: I arrived in Holland. I can't remember the name of the little village. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I was billeted with a burgermeister. It was then I became aware of what the Holocaust meant. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I met a non-Jewish lady with a family. And she said, we had-- we hid a-- a Jew-- a Jewish man with us. Unfortunately, he died in our-- in our home. We were terrified. In case they can't do anything more to him, they could put us in a camp. So we took the body out in the middle of the night and left it in the street and said, this is a Jew. Give him a Jewish burial. What happened, I don't know. Then, I began to learn bits and pieces. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Friday night, I never went out. On Shabbos, I never went out. Friday night, about 22:11, knock at the door. I opened it. There was a soldier there with fierce looking dog and signal, sir. So I opened the signal, Jewish soldier, badly wounded, asking for the chaplain. So I got my [INAUDIBLE] driver, come on, Harry, we're going. This-- we had to go to Eindhoven, not too far away.

speaker: interviewer
text: We arrived there about 10 to 12:00, 12 o'clock. I went to see the soldier. I stayed with him for a couple of hours, and the doctor came around and said, I think he'll survive until the morning. Go and have a sleep. Where do I sleep? On the floor near there. So I slept on the floor. And in the morning, you know, staff changes, another doctor came who was a Jewish man, whom I knew from Manchester. And-- so I said, tell me, are there any Jews? We've been reading that all-- all the Jews have been massacred. He-- he said, I know there's a Jewish-- I know a Jewish family. I'll take-- I'm off at 1 o'clock. I'll take you. So I had the presence of mind to say, stop. Wait till Shabbos is out. I don't know whether they're religious or not. Whether this terrible experience of theirs has brought back their faith or driven it out. I don't know. But I'm representing God the way I picture myself in their mind. Let's wait till after Shabbos. For the moment, I'd forgotten it was Hanukkah. As I entered the house, I said to them, [SPEAKING HEBREW]. They started to cry. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's over two years since we've [SPEAKING HEBREW]. Have you got any? Of course, we haven't got any candles. So I told my driver, go back to my headquarters. I've got a big steel case and bring as many candles as you can. Meanwhile, the elder daughter with her three children went around collecting many Jews who was-- who had been liberated.

speaker: interviewee
text: By the time my driver came back, there was about six or 10 families there. And they made a little cup of tea and a few cakes and so on. And we sang a few songs and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in 1944?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1944, 1944, Hanukkah, December. Was it December? Must have been 1944, yes, D-day was 1944. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There were only two children there. I said, I thought you had three children? Well, the little girl, she's with the nuns. I said, with what? With nuns. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Would you please bring her back? I'll send my truck for her. And the mother says, shh, be quiet. My husband hates her. I said, what are you talking about? She represents to him the angel of death. Please explain, I asked. She was born in the-- in the hospital there in Eindhoven just before another razzia, you know, a hunt for Jews was organized. And the-- the-- the members of the underground movement or whatever they called themselves came into the ward and said, get out as quickly as you can. They had foreseen certain things of this kind happening, So they had bought or rented a couple rooms at the top of a house just like the Anne Frank story. She wasn't the only one. And so when this order came, and said, we've got to get out. They took the baby, only days old, and they went into this particular place in-- into hiding. The kid had to cry. They can't stop it. She was very lucky she wasn't suffocated. More than once the father held his hand over her mouth. And anyhow as I said, she represented to him as being the angel of death. Even after the war, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she had that-- she had that representation of her. So as soon as they got into a home or their own home-- house again, he sent her away. He wouldn't allow her to eat at the table. Even when I went there two years later, he wouldn't-- she-- he wouldn't allow her to sit at the table. Anyhow, I said look, I'm coming every Friday night. I'll bring you food because I've got to eat too, and I'm not gonna have your rations, but I want the baby to be there. She wasn't there. I said, I'm not coming anymore, and you're not gonna get any more food. So I sent my truck, and the following week, she was there. The third, fourth week, she still wasn't there. And I couldn't send my truck anymore. There was a Canadian boy, and he said-- as I wrote her later, I said, get your commanding officer to-- to give you permission to bring the baby, which she did eventually. That kid-- no, her sister was here with her husband about two months-- two months ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: And right then, we moved on. We moved on. Other's fancy stories, moving-- moving stories, fanciful stories, incredible stories in Holland. And we moved on. And then I said to-- as we were moving into Germany, I said, I'm going back to Holland. I'm not doing any work. I visited a hospital, and I saw the non-Jewish chaplain dishing out, distributing cookies, razor blades, cigarettes, socks, all sorts of things, you know, in a big basket. I said, where'd you get this from? He says, well, you're at HQ, the welfare officer. Oh? So I go back to the welfare and said, why can't I get any? It's only for hospital chaplains. But we Jews cover everything. We haven't got enough men to cover hospitals alone. So he gave me. I pick out a cellophane packet of sweets, on which was written, this is a gift from the Rotary Club Havana, Cuba. A strange feeling came over me. I-- I can't believe any British Tom is gonna sit down and write a letter of thanks. And I did it. Here's the letter. Dated the 3rd of December, 1944. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Yes. Can I read it? Certainly.

speaker: interviewee
text: 'The president of the Rotary Club of Havana, Cuba. It's my pleasure and privilege to write to you on behalf of the lads of the British of the raiding army, who I've had the honor to serve to express our most grateful thanks and appreciation for the packets of sweets, et cetera, you most graciously sent us. So far as the sweets, or should it be designated candy, is concerned, there is no need for me to say how delicious they are. But we do most sincerely appreciate the thought behind the gift, for this too, we say thank you. Many of us belong to the various branches of the British Rotary Clubs, which in these times, the purpose of their existence only gains in tremendous significance. In the great battles which are being waged for liberty, freedom, and righteousness for all nations, the international humanitarianism of you aims and objects stand out above all else. Under the present conditions, we become more and more men of action, and less and less a vociferous verbiage. With this thought, allow me to terminate this letter, reiterating our ever gracious thanks to all those grand, warm-hearted people who made possible the distribution of these packets of candy. As it is the request of the authorities that I do not disclose any particulars of my unit, I regret that I cannot give you my army address. I can tell you that I'm somewhere on the continent, proud of being a member of the British Liberation Army. Therefore, I have given you my private home address in England, from where any letters will be forwarded to me here. After seeing with my own eyes and hearing with my own ears of the horrors these poor people have underwent-- have undergone at the hands of the loathsome Germans, and in particular the SS thugs, I can only say the world must see to it that such a barbarous government should never arise again.' I moved into Belsen. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] As they got on their feet, I said, you must have relations all over the world. You-- many of you may never have seen them. Give me a name. If you haven't got the full address, just the country or the town, and we'll send them. It wasn't easy. The authorities didn't like it in the beginning, but we got them through. Then, two sisters, [INAUDIBLE] came to me. We have a brother who escaped from Poland. They said, we believe he is in Havana.

speaker: interviewer
text: Something must have happened to my memory. I couldn't think for the life of me where Havana was. I remember America. I remember Canada and Australia, England. Where's Havana? Who to my-- to whom shall I send this letter? Again, God seemed to have guided me. I was looking through my files, and I came across this letter. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I got another letter. It appears that the people who contributed were not the Rotary Club people, but the girls of a high school. When the president got my letter, he handed it over to the head girl, they were thrilled. So when she wrote to me, I thought well, in that case, I'll write another letter to her personally. Then she wanted to-- I shall become a pen chum. She wanted a photograph, and I said, I've done my duty, enough. And I left the letter in my file. And I had occasion to open that file. I saw her letter. So I sent her letter to my wife, please send this letter, this urgent appeal to this girl. She's got to find this man. I don't care what she does, but find him. And she found-- found him, and they-- they became united. And then now, the-- one of the sisters died, unfortunately. Through natural causes, it's 50 years. And the other one is in Toronto. So she heard of me when she went to Israel for a visit, somebody was talking about where do you come from? London. Do you remember-- do you remember how it went. So she wrote to me. So the contact was-- was-- was made again. So that's a remarkable thing. Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have to go to that hospital, see the chaplain, complain that I didn't-- I didn't get a packet of these cookies, and then I had to send the letters of thanks. Extraordinary. Anyhow!

speaker: interviewer
text: So you got to Belsen April the 16th?

speaker: interviewee
text: April 16. I got-- yes, that was on a Monday, that's right. I was there for three hours. It was-- there's nothing I could do. And they were telling--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your first impression of the place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was stunned. I was stunned. First, well they wouldn't let me in. The guards were told, let nobody in. Anyhow, I told them who I was, and they got my identification card and so on. Anyhow, as I moved into the gates, there was a young woman there. At least, she looked an old woman, dirty, filthy, smelly. And I wanted to draw back. But then she saw the Magen David on my uniform, and she wanted to hug me. I was scared. Luckily, I controlled myself, otherwise she would've fallen flat on her face. And we walked further.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who-- who was with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Myself, this girl, this woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, 'cause you said we [? walked-- ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: The girl with me, yes. Myself and-- and-- and the girl. Then I saw about six or eight bodies lying on the-- people lying on the floor. And this-- it was a sunny day. Again, I said, why are they-- why are they not sleeping in the huts near around? Why are they sleeping here? She says, their dead. And we'll all be dead if we don't get proper food and medicines. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Walked further into the camp. My gosh, as if the dead were walking. [BACKROUND NOISE] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They weren't-- as if their bones were kept together by sinews of skin. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Again, she said, they're not dead now, but they will be. And I talked to a group of women that-- apparently we went towards the women's camp. The women were separated from the men in-- in the camps by the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then one woman was going like this. Pointing to a woman in-- in crowd, but the woman herself couldn't see it. I said, what do you mean? We spoke in Yiddish. I think she's mentally deranged. She saw her child being murdered. They threw the kid up and see how many bullets got into his body before it dropped. What could I do? I'm no psychiatrist. Oh my gosh. Anyhow, I felt I had to go back to the army, saying that I-- I must come back here. Whether you give permission or not, I've got to come here. So at least my colonel gave-- How did you-- how did you get there in the first place?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- oh. A Sunday afternoon-- Sunday afternoon, I arrived at [? Sello. ?] That's where my HQ was. I go into the camp. I go into the mess. Silence could be cut like a knife. In-- in a-- in a village anyhow on a Sunday afternoon is usually quiet. Nobody would talk to me. The officers turned their backs on me. I said, what is?

speaker: interviewer
text: These were British officers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. Anti-semitism. What have I done, have I got the plague? Anyhow, one of the left tenants said go and see the colonel [? apparently. ?] I went to see the colonel. He offered me a drink, which was an amazing thing. I said, no sir. He didn't look well. I said, what's the matter, you're not well? We've just uncovered a concentration camp, and it's horrible. Most of the people I believe are your people. You better go there. They will need you. Shall I go now, sir? No, go tomorrow morning. And I drove, and I drove. It was in about 16 miles. Or 16 kilometers-- no, 16 miles. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and on the sides, you know, there was a colonnade of trees. And they were beginning to stick up notices, death, typhus, keep out, keep out. Death, typhus. I didn't know typhus from-- from a belly ache, whatever it was. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then we went in. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: God, what could I do? What could I do? Just talking to-- just talking. I couldn't talk to the dying or dead. The dying, I said the Shema. And I closed their eyes, if-- if they still had eyes. If she still had eyes. Hoo-hoo. [INAUDIBLE] the army-- the army had started helping with food. They gave up their own rations. And the bully beef, it was too tough, too hard for them. They couldn't digest it, many died. But I believe that many of them would've died anyhow.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think we could blame the army, not that they had any-- any other way to-- of helping themselves. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I go into-- to huts, terrible. Dysentery, diarrhea, no proper latrines. The girls are becoming feministic again or-- they're become-- they were dry, dehydrated. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then their monthly periods started to come. They had no sanitary towels. That was one of the first letters I sent back to Leeds, regard send-- send sanitary towels.

speaker: interviewer
text: I tried to use bits and papers of cloth or paper to clean them up, then one girl said, no, it's not nice. I mean, she had the sense of dignity. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then I go around-- we'd just go around and talking. What else can you do? I can't remember details. Then the-- a padre came in, an RAF Jewish fellow. That's right, he was a minister in Leeds too, Louis. Don't know where he is now. I said, Louis, I'm oblivious of what's going on outside. For God's sake, take me somewhere so I can get help. There's a Canadian Air Force unit not too far away. Good, take me there. So I went to the-- see the padre. He took me to the doctor, the medical officer. I said, I'm ready to ransack the whole of my medical chest, over 2,000 men were on there, so you can't do that. I'm doing it. And I went to the CO. They were just having a meal. He said, would you like to talk to the officers now? I said, let them have their main meal. I'll talk to them just before they have their sweets. Because if I talk, they will not eat their meal.

speaker: interviewee
text: I've never seen two officers get up like they got up. One says, I swear, I'm going out on a-- on an air mission of bombing. I promise you I will not bring my bombs back. I didn't know whether it was legal to say that, I don't know. But that's how he said it. And one [? said, ?] I-- we-- I volunteer two days' bread ration. Then I spoke to nearly 2,000 ordinary airmen. They all volunteered their cigarettes and everything else. They had little trinkets they bought for their wives or sweethearts or mothers. They were willing to offer those things. And for two or three days, two or three trucks came in regularly with bread, and this and that and the other. And I took obviously to the kitchens where-- where the food was to-- was distributed. And there was an officer, Leftenant Derek Sington. He was the psychological warfare officer at the time. He arranged the handing over of the-- of the camp and the arrest of the Nazis there. And in his book, the first book ever written on Belsen, by the [? Duckwood ?] Press, he said, what was needed more than anything else was proper foods. And Captain Hardman got it. He said, I don't know where they got it from in his book-- I've got a copy of the-- of his book there. And then I was warned, don't give them more than the official ration. I thought I was doing them harm when they kept on shouting, hunger, [NON-ENGLISH] hunger, hunger! When a kid asks and he cried hunger, you can't-- you can't say no straight away. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] So I fed them. And this Derek--

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people where there--

speaker: interviewee
text: There were--

speaker: interviewer
text: --needing to be fed?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was an average of about 12,000. 12,000 were dead. Between 10,000 or 12,000 were dead, and there must have been about 6,000 or 8,000 alive-- no more, more. 13,000 died after the liberation. There was 10,000 to 15,000 dead when we came in. And 13,000 died after the liberation. So there must have been many, many thousand. Then a Dr. [? Meiklejohn. ?] He was famous for his Bengal Famine Mixture, the British government sent him out to Bengal. And he came to me, and he said, padre, they're not eating my food.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, forgive me, sir, what I'm going to say. You've never had this experience. What do you mean? I've just-- I've been-- just come back from Bengal. I said, let me try to explain myself. When a person's upset, a businessmen, something upsets him, the secretary, or his partners, or his customers, the gastric juices don't appear to be in-- in order. And he'll come home and his wife will plate something in front of him. I can't eat that. Or she gives him something, it gives him pains. So it's about when you're upset, it ups the acid, and these people were upset. You can imagine. How can they-- Yes, I see that. Secondly, they come from Eastern Europe, which I still believe is different from living in Bengal. The climate affects your gastric juices. Yes, I see that to a certain extent. And I looked at him. Forgive me for what I'm going to say now, sir. What? They're Jews-- Oh. He jumped three feet in the air. Hell are you talking about? As in, they come from-- I don't know how exactly to explain this, and we have a little shtickle herring, translated in Yiddish, a little-- a little sour, you know, something. Your-- your stuff is apparently too sweet. He said, I can't give them acid. I said, I never said acid. We've had a couple of postmortems and their intestines are red hot. I said, I'm not talking about that. I said, try something else.

speaker: interviewee
text: He tried it. There was to a certain extent satisfied, but then it was moved. They moved [? Meiklejohn's ?] food mixture. Then I went on feeding them. And he says, be careful padre, you're not-- you don't attend daily conferences which you should. They're talking about you. I get on an active service, remember, anything can happen. Thank you very much, and I went on feeding them. And became more and more closely lit-- knit with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then Richard Dimbleby, you know, the famous broadcaster came in. [LAUGHS] He came in-- it was a-- a week later. He said, could you hold a service? I said, for whom? Well, for these people and for-- for the British public. Want in Hebrew or English? I'm not used to this kind of presentation of the service. He said, do something. He announced that it was on the 15th of April, and it was a week later. I said, it's up to you, sir. I won't tell anybody.

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, he was busy occupied with doing other work. It's up to you whether you say you-- you-- you arrived on that day. So-- oh, we got a number of people together. The men couldn't come. There were-- women, somehow, were able to get on their feet much quicker than men. And there were two-- two girls, Esther Iskovitz and Shoshannah Iskovitz, I think, from Czechoslovakia. Learned, cultured, English speaking. Hebrew speaking. German speaking. They got jobs for the military because they needed interpreters. And they sang a beautiful Adon Olam, which we haven't got here. I lent it out to somebody. That's what I talked about before. I thought-- I thought he'd bring it back so you can hear it here. And the elder sister, Esther, died four weeks later. She was ill. I didn't know what. And fortunately, the British medical officer came, also a friend of mine from Liverpool. I said, Basil, go and have a look at her. He said, I don't know what's wrong her, Les. Just don't know. She died. I had a terrible feeling. If I'm wrong, I asked people to forgive me.

speaker: interviewer
text: A-- English nurses were not allowed in the early days. It was too horrific, too dirty, too horrible. So they-- they-- they brought in German nurses. Whether they were proper nurses, I don't know. But when this kid-- this girl, Esther, took ill, she had a nurse with her. One of the German nurses. Did she kill her? Did she inject her with poison? We don't know. And it was the first individual burial I participated in. We dug a grave. It was not easy, no shrouds, no coffins, no Torah, just put the body in dirt.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then the other girl, she married a very prominent doctor now at [INAUDIBLE] in America. [INAUDIBLE]. But they lost their Yiddish kind. They lost their faith, their Jewishness. You can't lose faith. Wherever God reigns, you can't lose faith. You must have faith, you can't walk across the road without having faith. But obviously, those are religious details, the ritualistic observances. And she got married, and she had two sons. And they came over here. And she sent them to St. Albans which was a very posh American, Anglo-American school, which means they-- they weren't interested in-- in Jewishness. [INAUDIBLE] great pity. And I haven't been able to see her since. So that's when gradually, gradually, we had services. And--
